inputs.label: Annual Demand (units/year)
inputs.businessContext: Total yearly demand for the product. Used to calculate order frequency and inventory holding costs. Based on EOQ model principles.

inputs.label: Ordering Cost per Order (USD)
inputs.businessContext: Fixed cost incurred per purchase order (e.g., administrative, shipping). Used in EOQ formula.

inputs.label: Holding Cost per Unit per Year (USD)
inputs.businessContext: Cost to hold one unit of inventory for one year (storage, insurance, obsolescence). Used in EOQ formula.

inputs.label: Unit Cost (USD)
inputs.businessContext: Purchase cost per unit. Used to calculate total inventory value and financial exposure.

inputs.label: Minimum Order Quantity (units)
inputs.businessContext: Supplier-imposed minimum quantity per order. May force order sizes above EOQ, increasing holding costs.

inputs.label: Lead Time (days)
inputs.businessContext: Time from placing order to receipt. Affects safety stock and reorder point.

inputs.label: Safety Stock (units)
inputs.businessContext: Extra inventory held to buffer against demand variability. Increases holding costs.

inputs.label: Working Days per Year
inputs.businessContext: Number of operating days per year. Used to convert annual demand to daily demand.

rules: If moq > annualDemand, then warning: MOQ exceeds annual demand
If holdingCostPerUnit > unitCost * 0.5, then warning: holding cost is high relative to unit cost

formulas.decisionVerdict: variancePercent > 50 ? 'Negotiate MOQ reduction' : variancePercent > 20 ? 'Consider MOQ adjustment' : 'Accept current MOQ'

breakdown.totalInventoryCost: Sum of holding and ordering costs (USD)
breakdown.variancePercent: Percentage deviation from EOQ due to MOQ

hiddenLossDrivers: If moq > eoq: excess inventory due to MOQ constraint
If safetyStock > 0.5 * averageInventory: high safety stock relative to cycle stock
suggestedActions: If variancePercent > 20: negotiate lower MOQ with supplier
If holdingCostPerUnit > unitCost * 0.3: review storage efficiency or reduce safety stock
If orderingCost is high: consider consolidating orders or using blanket orders
